result:
The silence, they say, is the hardest part. But that’s not quite right. The silence is a canvas. The noise—the traffic, the notifications, the internal hum of *what’s-next-what’s-next*—that is the hard part. The noise is the static that obscures the picture. So here, in this quiet space of 1000 words, I find not a prison, but a clearing.

It makes me think of mornings. Not the frantic, alarm-clanging ones, but the rare, stolen ones. Where you wake before the world has drawn its first sharp breath, and the light is the colour of undiluted honey. In that stillness, you are not a person with a to-do list; you are a creature simply being. You feel the cool air on your skin, watch a single dust mote spiral in a sunbeam, and for a moment, you are aligned with something vast and gentle. That’s what this blank page feels like. A pre-dawn world.

We spend our lives building narratives. We are the heroes, the victims, the side-characters in our own epic, tragic, or comedic films. We curate our stories for résumés, for first dates, for the mirror. We sand down the rough edges of a bad day and varnish a small victory until it shines with implied greater meaning. But here, in the raw unbuilding of a free-flow of thought, the narrative frays. And in that fraying is a strange honesty.

What comes to me is the memory of my grandfather’s hands. They were maps of a life of labour: rivers of blue veins, topographical lines of scars, nails permanently etched with fine black lines, like charcoal drawings. He could fix a watch with the delicate precision of a surgeon, yet those same hands could split firewood with a single, thunderous blow. I think of how he’d hold a tomato from his garden, cradling its redness with a reverence we reserve for holy objects. He never said much. But his hands told stories of creation and repair, of patience and force. We’ve lost that. Our hands now are instruments of tapping and swiping, leaving no physical imprint, building nothing we can hold that has weight and grain. We create digital ghosts. He grew food that tasted of sun and soil. I wonder which of us was richer.

This thought spirals into another: the tyranny of convenience. Everything is at our fingertips—any song, any fact, any person, any meal. But with the removal of friction, we have also removed texture. The joy of anticipation, the struggle that makes the arrival sweet, the accidental discovery in the search for something else. We have streamlined serendipity out of existence. I miss the *wait*. I miss the forgotten song that suddenly plays on a crackly radio, flooding you with a memory you didn’t know you’d stored. I miss the dusty library shelf that offers you the wrong, perfect book. Our algorithms know us too well; they show us only mirrors, never windows.

And yet, here I am, using one of the ultimate tools of this age to yearn for a past I only partly knew. The contradiction doesn’t escape me. We are all living in this permanent tension, this low-grade nostalgia for a present we’re too busy to inhabit. We document the concert through our phone screens, watching the pixels dance while the real, earth-shaking bass vibrates through our bodies, ignored. We are haunted by the fear of missing out, so we miss out on what is directly in front of us.

Perhaps what I’m circling is a plea for attention. Not the kind we demand from others, but the kind we owe to our own lives. The poet Mary Oliver asked: “Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” The question isn’t about grand achievements. It’s about the quality of your attention. Will you see the way the rain beads on a spiderweb? Will you really listen to the cadence of a friend’s laughter? Will you taste your food, or just consume it?

The world is on fire in so many ways—with actual flames, with rhetoric, with anxiety. The grand problems feel insoluble, a weight that pins us to our beds. But maybe the rebellion is in the small, fierce act of noticing. Of being present. It is a quiet defiance to plant a basil plant on your windowsill and watch it grow. To mend a sock instead of throwing it away. To write a letter by hand, the ink forming clumsy, personal shapes. To sit with someone in grief, offering not solutions, but the solid, silent architecture of your presence.

My 1000 words are running out. They have been a meander, not a march. There is no thesis here, only a series of footsteps in the dew of this morning-page. And that feels appropriate. We are not equations to be solved; we are mysteries to be lived.

So I will end with this: look up. Right now, from wherever you are reading this. Find one thing you hadn’t noticed before. The pattern in the ceiling tile, the particular green of a leaf against the grey sky, the way shadows fall across your keyboard. For that one moment, be there. Fully.

That moment of attention is a seed. It doesn’t solve the world’s chaos. But it roots you in your own existence. And from that rooted place, who knows what might grow? Perhaps kindness. Perhaps clarity. Perhaps just the quiet strength to face another day.

The silence after these words will not be empty. It will be waiting.
